text.title: Problem Validation 2026 Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Problem Validation 2026 application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every interview MUST start with the 5-step opener (Vision-state → Framing-honesty → Weakness-admit → Pedestal-knock → Ask for help). Skipping or reordering steps is rejected.
rule.rationale: The opener sequence drops interviewee performance pressure; reordered openers leave the pedestal intact and answers stay flattering.

rule.statement: Every evidence item MUST be tagged with a commitment-signal type: Time | Reputation | Money. Untagged evidence is rejected.
rule.rationale: Mapping evidence tier to commitment signal makes the hierarchy operational — interviewers know what behaviour to look for at each tier.

rule.statement: The artefact MUST schedule the next iteration (target date, target N, target tier upgrade). One-shot validations are rejected.
rule.rationale: Validation is a continuous learning loop, not a gate; a one-shot artefact decays in days.

rule.statement: For every Mom-Test-failing question used historically, a replacement past-behaviour question MUST be recorded in a replacement table. Untracked rewrites lose institutional memory.
rule.rationale: Without a replacement table, the team rewrites the same bad questions across sprints.

rule.statement: The artefact MUST name a single accountable owner per iteration. "the team" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Weekly cadence dies without a named owner; ownership rotates only if explicitly handed off in writing.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
